nodes.title: Back stack with an array
nodes.why: A stack only needs add/remove at one end, and arrays handle that well.
nodes.tradeoff: Array code is simpler; linked lists avoid occasional array resizing but add complexity.

nodes.title: Use push/pop for LIFO behavior
nodes.why: `push` and `pop` naturally model stack behavior: last in, first out.
nodes.tradeoff: End-based operations are O(1) amortized; front operations are slower.

nodes.title: Track queue front with a head pointer
nodes.why: This avoids `shift()` and keeps dequeue fast even for large queues.
nodes.tradeoff: Head pointer is a bit more state, but avoids O(n) reindexing each dequeue.

nodes.title: Return undefined when queue is empty
nodes.why: Empty dequeue should be safe and predictable, not an error.
nodes.alternative: Throw an exception on empty dequeue.
nodes.tradeoff: `undefined` is easy for callers, but they must remember to check for it.

nodes.title: Read then advance head
nodes.why: This keeps dequeue O(1) because no elements are moved.
nodes.tradeoff: Much faster for long queues, but old consumed items stay in memory until compaction.

nodes.title: Compact storage after many dequeues
nodes.why: Compaction drops consumed items so the internal array does not grow forever.
nodes.alternative: Never compact and rely on occasional full resets.
nodes.tradeoff: Compaction does occasional O(n) work, but keeps memory usage bounded.
